content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of length NNN, which initially is a permutation of integers from 111 to NNN.

We want to make the array empty by performing the following operation NNN times: choose any index iii and remove AiA_iAi​ from AAA, which decrements the size of AAA by 111.

We will perform exactly NNN such operations. Apparently, there are N!N!N! ways to do this. But now we wonder how many ways satisfy that, during the process, the array doesn't contain any consecutive monotone triples?

A consecutive monotone triple is a tuple of consecutive elements (i,i+1,i+2)(i, i+1, i+2)(i,i+1,i+2)
which satisfy

Ai<Ai+1<Ai+2A_i < A_{i+1} < A_{i+2}Ai​<Ai+1​<Ai+2​ or Ai>Ai+1>Ai+2A_i > A_{i+1} > A_{i+2}Ai​>Ai+1​>Ai+2​.

Since the answer may be large, print it modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.


Input Format
The first line contains a single integer TTT −-− the number of test cases.


The first line of each test case contains the single integer NNN.


The second line of each testcases contains nnn integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ −-− the initial array AAA.



Output Format
For each test case print a single integer −-− the answer modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases doesn't exceed 200 000200\,000200000.
Initially AAA doesn't contain consecutive monotone triples.
**Subtask 1 (50 points):** The sum of NNN over all test cases doesn't exceed 500050005000.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
In the second testcase, an example of a valid deletion is [1,4,2,3]→[1,4,3]→[1,3]→[1][1,4,2,3] \rightarrow [1,4,3] \rightarrow  [1,3] \rightarrow  [1][1,4,2,3]→[1,4,3]→[1,3]→[1].
Meanwhile [1,4,2,3]→[1,2,3]→[1,3]→[1][1,4,2,3] \rightarrow [1,2,3] \rightarrow  [1,3] \rightarrow  [1][1,4,2,3]→[1,2,3]→[1,3]→[1] is not a valid deletion since [1,2,3][1,2,3][1,2,3] contains a consectutive monoton triple.
